title: Attribution Models: Same Journey, Different Conclusions
text:
The Problem

  
    
      
    
    Facebook Ad
  
  →
  
    
      
    
    Google Ads
  
  →
  
    📧
    Email Click
  
  →
  
    💰
    Purchase
  


The data aren’t good enough to tell us who gets credit.


So we pick a model — and assign credit as close to reality as we can.

title: Attribution Models: Same Journey, Different Conclusions
text:
5 Attribution Models

  
    First-touch
    100% to first touch
  
  
    Last-touch
    100% to last touch
  
  
    Linear
    Equal split across all
  
  
    Time Decay
    More credit near conversion
  
  
    U-shape
    40% · 20% · 40%
  











Facebook credit: 0 → 1,000 depending on model chosen. Same data. Very different story.

title: Attribution Models: Same Journey, Different Conclusions
text:
The Numbers Can Diverge Drastically
Imagine 1,000 conversions across Facebook, Google, Email










Facebook credit: 50 → 420 depending on model chosen. Same data. Very different story.

title: Attribution Models: Same Journey, Different Conclusions
text:
The Numbers Can Diverge Drastically
Imagine 1,000 conversions across Facebook, Google, Email










Facebook credit: 50 → 420 depending on model chosen.

title: Attribution Models: Same Journey, Different Conclusions
text:
Beware: Models Can Mislead

  
    First-touch → Makes the first channel look like a hero.
    But did it really cause the conversion — or just happen to be first?
  
  
    Last-touch → Makes the last channel look like a hero.
    But the customer might have already decided to buy before that click.
  
  
    Any model → Can make a channel seem more or less important than it really is.
    The model doesn't reveal the truth — it just assigns credit.

title: Attribution Models: Same Journey, Different Conclusions
text:
So What’s the Real Solution?

Attribution models are educated guesses.
Without better data, you’re just picking a story.


The real answer? A/B Testing.


Randomly assign customers to see — or not see — each touchpoint. Only then can you measure the true causal effect of each channel. Without it, you’re choosing a model and hoping for the best.

title: Attribution Models: Same Journey, Different Conclusions
section: How Do We Know Which Model Is Better?
text:
How Do We Know Which Model Is Better?

  
    💡 Spend Validation
    
      
        ✅ Good Model
        
          
          
          Spend
          ── Attribution
        
        Spend ↑ → Attribution ↑
      
      
        ❌ Poor Model
        
          
          
          Spend
          ── Attribution
        
        Spend fluctuates → Attribution flat
      
    
  
  
    🧪 Incrementality Testing
    Randomly withhold a channel from a control group. Measure the conversion difference.
    A form of A/B testing — the gold standard for measuring true channel impact.
  


Both methods help us move from assuming to validating.

title: Attribution Models: Same Journey, Different Conclusions
text:
Takeaway

The data alone can’t tell us who drove the sale.
So we pick a model — and validate it.


Use spend validation as a quick sanity check. Use incrementality testing when you need the real answer.


Attribution models assign credit. Only experiments can prove it.
